# Forensic Dossier - Hera S.p.A. (HER.MI)

*A deep dive on management and the setup. Multi-utility (Italy, Emilia-Romagna); flagged as deep value on a conservative book-value method.*
*Source basis: No bundled earnings transcript was available. Reconstructed from the first-quarter 2026 earnings call (investing.com transcript, May 2026), the full-year 2025 results presentation, the 2025-2029 business plan, and screener financials. Figures are dated; no quotes were fabricated.*

## 1. Narrative arc

Hera's story has gone through a deliberate, management-engineered rotation from a commodity-energy narrative to a regulated-network one, and that arc is the single most important thing in this name.

- **2021-2022 (the spike):** Revenue exploded from EUR 10.6B (2021) to EUR 19.9B (2022) on the European gas-price shock. This was a price-driven, low-quality top line - energy trading and last-resort supply inflated revenue while net income barely moved (EUR 334M to EUR 255M; earnings per share actually fell from 0.23 to 0.18). The narrative at the time was about resilience through the energy crisis.
- **2023-2024 (the unwind):** Revenue collapsed back to EUR 15.4B and then EUR 13.1B as commodity prices normalized. Crucially, net income rose into the unwind (EUR 441M to EUR 494M), proving the 2022 revenue was froth. Management began emphasizing the rebalancing toward networks.
- **2025 to first quarter 2026 (the pivot completed):** Revenue settled at EUR 12.8B. The full-year 2025 narrative explicitly celebrates that networks rose from 32% to 38% of group operating profit while energy fell from 42% to 35% - nearly in line with the targets set in the business plan through 2029, three years ahead of schedule. The first-quarter 2026 call reframes the whole business around regulated rate-base growth, water and waste acquisitions, and structural rather than temporary earnings.

The dropped driver is explicit and honest: management openly retired the last-resort energy-supply tailwind, quantifying a roughly EUR 180M full-year operating-profit headwind from its fade and EUR 33M of lost opportunities in the first quarter alone. The introduced driver is rate-base-financed network growth plus bolt-on acquisitions in circular-economy waste (Aliplast, ACR Reggiani, Ambiente Energia, Sostelia, and lifting SEA to 83%).

**Net:** the arc is a credible de-risking story - trading a volatile commodity lever for a regulated annuity - executed ahead of plan. This is the opposite of a deteriorating narrative.

## 2. Claims versus financials (forensic)

| Management claim | Financial evidence | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| "Energy continues to grow, +24%" (first-quarter 2026 operating profit) | First-quarter group operating profit was only up 0.2% to EUR 418.9M; energy's 24% is offsetting the roughly EUR 180M last-resort-energy fade | True but selective - the energy segment grew, but it is masking a managed wind-down; group operating profit is flat, not growing |
| Portfolio rebalanced to 38% networks / 35% energy, "three years ahead of schedule" | Full-year 2025 Networks EUR 591M (38%), Energy EUR 540M (35%), Waste EUR 374M - the math checks out | True and corroborated |
| "Free cash flow nearly doubled to EUR 761M" (full-year 2025) | Screener trailing free cash flow is only EUR 421M (as of 31 March 2026); the EUR 761M figure includes a working-capital reversal from the commodity unwind | True but non-recurring - the doubling is a one-off, commodity-driven working-capital release; normalized free cash flow is about EUR 420M, consistent with about 26 times free cash flow |
| Net debt relative to operating profit "within comfort zone" at 2.57 times (full-year 2025) rising to 2.62 times (first quarter 2026) | Net debt EUR 4.18B (recomputed: EUR 5.52B debt minus EUR 1.33B cash). Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 1.4 (optically in the distress zone, but normal for a capital-intensive regulated utility) | True - leverage is utility-normal; rising on acquisitions and capital spending, still below the three-times plan ceiling |
| Capital spending up 24% to EUR 237.7M, "fully self-financed" | First-quarter operating cash flow of EUR 315M exceeded EUR 240M of capital spending plus acquisitions; free cash flow positive | True - growth is internally funded, with no equity dilution signaled |
| Dividend raised to EUR 0.16 (up 6.7%), "22 consecutive years" | Confirmed; trades without the dividend from 22 June 2026, paid 24 June 2026 - pending, not yet paid | True - an ordinary annual rise, not a special dividend |

**Where the story is richer than the numbers:** the "+24% energy" headline and the "free cash flow doubled" framing flatter a group that is fundamentally flat-to-low-single-digit growth (group operating profit up 0.2% in the first quarter; full-year 2025 operating profit of EUR 1,537M was actually down from EUR 1,588M reported, and up only against an adjusted EUR 1,474M base). Management is narrating a transition, and the transition is real, but the underlying organic growth is modest.

**Where the story is poorer than the numbers deserve:** the screener's gross margin of 5.7% and net margin of 3.6% look thin, but that is a revenue-denominator artifact - Hera's revenue is gross of pass-through energy commodity costs, so the margins on the value-added regulated and waste base are far healthier than the optics suggest. The 5.6% return on invested capital is the more honest read, and the 2029 plan targets about 9.3%.

## 3. Tone and confidence trajectory

**Trajectory: stable to rising (a confident blend of offense and defense).**

- First quarter 2026, on commodity normalization: forward pricing implies average growth between electricity and gas of roughly 15%, which executive chairman Fabbri characterized as not especially worrisome for the future - measured, not alarmed.
- On competition and customer churn: margins "can stay in line with the ones we've always had," and churn is "lower than the market average" - confident on retention.
- On the plan: "one-fourth of the goals we've given ourselves in the business plan, which means we are ahead of schedule" - explicitly on the front foot.
- On acquisitions: these are "industrial players which we can add to our industrial portfolio," with emphasis on "extracting synergies in a very determined way" - conviction on capital allocation.

There is no shift from confident to defensive language; rather, management pre-empted the defensive question (the fading last-resort energy supply) by quantifying it and showing the offsets. Both top executives (Fabbri and Orazio) were reconfirmed for a further three years during the period - a structural signal of confidence.

## 4. Guidance credibility

**Reliability: high.**

- The 2025 results came in three years ahead of schedule on the single most-watched plan metric (the portfolio mix), and the 2029 plan was revised upward across all five-year targets (operating profit to EUR 1.76B; dividend to EUR 0.19, up 27%; EUR 5.5B of capital spending). Raising long-range targets while delivering early is the signature of an under-promise, over-deliver culture - common among Italian regulated utilities.
- The EUR 180M last-resort-energy headwind was pre-disclosed and the offset plan articulated; management has a track record (correctly framing the 2022 froth as non-recurring, with net income rising into the revenue collapse) of being honest about the quality of earnings.
- There is no evidence of missed guidance in the trajectory; net income beat the revenue narrative consistently from 2022 to 2024.

## 5. Analyst pressure points

The recurring pressure points are energy-margin durability and acquisition discipline:
- Analysts probed the EUR 33M first-quarter and EUR 180M full-year loss of last-resort-energy opportunities - management did not deflect, quantifying it and pointing to the 24% energy-segment growth and synergy extraction as offsets.
- The 2.62 times net-debt-to-operating-profit creeping up on acquisitions is the live worry - management's answer ("self-financed," "within comfort zone," below the three-times plan ceiling) is credible but is the thing to watch if deals accelerate.
- The unaddressed analyst concern is the valuation premium itself: at about 26 times free cash flow Hera screens as the most expensive Italian utility, roughly four times A2A's 7-8 times. Management does not address why the market should pay a premium beyond dividend stability and regulated dominance in Emilia-Romagna - a quality-of-income argument, not a growth one.

## 6. Red flags and green flags

Green flag: portfolio de-risked ahead of plan - networks at 38% of operating profit, three years early; lower volatility, higher quality of income.
Green flag: 2029 plan revised upward while delivering early - EUR 1.76B operating profit, dividend up 27%, return on invested capital toward 9.3%.
Green flag: self-funded growth - first-quarter capital spending and acquisitions covered by operating cash flow; no dilution; 22 consecutive years of dividends.
Green flag: management continuity - Fabbri and Orazio reconfirmed for three years; honest framing of earnings quality through the commodity cycle.
Green flag: free-cash-flow inflection - full-year 2025 free cash flow of EUR 761M (even if helped by working capital), enabling the dividend ramp and bolt-ons.

Red flag: the valuation premium to peers is the core risk - about 26 times free cash flow versus A2A's 7-8 times; the discount-to-peers comparison runs the wrong way, since Hera is the expensive one.
Red flag: group organic growth is flat - group operating profit up 0.2% in the first quarter, full-year 2025 operating profit down versus prior reported; the "growth" is mix-shift and acquisitions, not organic volume.
Red flag: normalized free cash flow (about EUR 421M trailing) is well below the EUR 761M headline - the doubling is partly a non-recurring, commodity-unwind working-capital release.
Red flag: net debt of EUR 4.18B and rising on acquisitions - Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 1.4 (utility-normal but with no cushion if rates or regulation turn); leverage creeping toward the three-times ceiling.
Red flag: margin is 0.32 standard deviations below its historical peak, with gross margin contracting per the scan.

## 7. Hidden signals

- The terminology pivot from "opportunities" to "industrial portfolio" is the tell: management has consciously stopped selling the energy-trading upside (which it is winding down) and reframed every acquisition as a durable, synergy-bearing industrial asset. This is a quality-of-earnings upgrade in the language, matched by the actual mix shift.
- "Structural" is now the load-bearing adjective - management repeatedly contrasts structural growth (rate base, waste plants, water concessions) against the retired temporary energy margin. The market has not fully re-rated the multiple to reflect the lower-volatility, higher-quality earnings stream - which is the entire bull case.
- The dividend cadence (trades without the dividend 22 June, paid 24 June 2026) is locked in and rising - no sign of a cut; the yield-focused Italian retail and institutional base is being fed, not flushed.
- The regulated rate base and the ARERA regulatory framework form a regulatory-license advantage that is widening via investment into the rate base, not eroding.

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict (for the director)

**Classification: stable, with a strengthening quality-of-earnings vector.**

Hera is a well-run, conservatively financed regulated multi-utility that has deliberately and successfully made its earnings base less risky - trading volatile energy-trading margin for a regulated network annuity, three years ahead of its own plan, while raising long-range targets and the dividend. This is a high-credibility, honest management team.

**The single thing the director must weigh before sizing:** the entire thesis is a valuation question, not a fundamental one. The business is excellent; the price is not cheap. At about 26 times normalized free cash flow Hera is the most expensive utility in its peer set (A2A around 7-8 times), and the deep-value tag rests on asset-replacement intrinsic value (the two conservative book-value yardsticks at 79% and 57% margins of safety), while three cash-flow-based valuations all read fully valued or worse. The asymmetry is therefore modest - this is a quality compounder at a full-to-rich multiple, not a mispriced deep-value sell-off. The 5% screener margin of safety and the price at 24% of the 52-week range (below the 200-day moving average) reflect a name that is cheap to itself recently but rich to its peers. Size it as a low-volatility quality holding, not as an asymmetric special situation.

**Competitive moat:** a regulatory license (regulated gas, electric, and water distribution overseen by ARERA, plus dominant waste concessions in Emilia-Romagna). Width: narrow-to-wide on the regulated core, but the blended return is modest - return on invested capital of 5.6% today is barely above the cost of capital, but it is rising toward a planned roughly 9.3% as the energy froth washes out and high-return regulated investment enters the rate base. This is a widening moat by trend (the mix shift toward higher-quality regulated assets), not an eroding one. Margins look thin only because of the pass-through revenue denominator.

**Longer-term threat: manageable.** Regulated water, waste, and gas-distribution networks are among the most defensible assets in the equity universe over time - local monopolies, inflation-linked tariffs, essential services. The energy transition is a tailwind here (circular-economy waste, water resilience, grid investment funded into the rate base), not a loser. No artificial-intelligence, fintech, cord-cutting, or autonomous-vehicle shift touches its long-run value. The only structural watch-item is Italian regulatory rate-of-return resets, which are a known, bounded, slow-moving risk.

In summary: credibility is high, the trajectory is stable, the moat is wide and widening, and the longer-term threat is manageable.
